Adding ال with a shadda makes it point to something specific
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. This word describes the people who disbelieved (كَفَرُوا۟) in this ayah.

steps.m: from (connecting قَوْمِهِۦ to what came before)
steps.n: Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group something belongs to. Here it shows the disbelievers were from 'his people'.
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'people'
Added i-sound (kasra) at the end to show this word belongs to or is owned by something

Basic demonstrative pronoun for pointing to something nearby (masculine singular)

Changed the final alif to alif with madda (آ) to show elongation in pronunciation

This is a particle that excludes what comes after it (بَشَرٌ - a human) from what came before (ٱلْمَلَؤُا۟ - the chiefs). In this ayah, it shows that the person being discussed is just a regular human, not someone special like the chiefs were claiming.

steps.m: to have outer appearance, to show on surface

steps.n: Adding the vowels creates a noun meaning the visible/apparent creation - humans

steps.n: This word connects two ideas - what someone wants and what they want to happen. In this ayah, it connects 'he wants' (يُرِيدُ) to 'to gain superiority' (يَتَفَضَّلَ), showing that gaining superiority is what he wants to do.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and doubled middle letter to show doing something deliberately for someone else's benefit

steps.m: that he bestows favor (object of يُرِيدُ أَن)
steps.n: Added يَ to show present tense. This verb comes after يُرِيدُ أَن (he wants that) in the ayah, showing what someone wants to do.
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
The ى changes to يْ when connecting to a pronoun
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce this word that introduces a hypothetical situation that didn't happen
Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before it. In this ayah, it connects to the disbelievers' previous statement, adding another point in their argument.

steps.m: to want or wish for something

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he wanted'
The weak letter hamza (ء) combines with alif to make a long vowel sound
Changed from 'a god' to 'The God' as a proper name by adding ال and merging letters

steps.m: Allah (as the subject of شَآءَ - willed/wished)
steps.n: Added the shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to hamzat wasl, and added damma to show this is the subject doing the action in شَآءَ (willed/wished)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ makes the verb causative - showing that someone is making the action happen. In this ayah, Allah would be causing angels (ملائكة) to be sent down.

steps.m: surely He would have sent down
steps.n: The لَ prefix adds emphasis and shows this is part of a conditional statement (with لَوْ - 'if' earlier in the ayah)

Makes the word plural by adding ا after first letter and ِ before last letter - common way to make plurals in Arabic

steps.m: angels (as the object being acted upon)
steps.n: Adds ً to show these angels are being acted upon in the sentence (in this case, what Allah would send down)
Basic negative particle that negates the verb يُرِيدُ (he wants) in this ayah
The shadda (double mark) on م adds emphasis to the negation, making it stronger like saying 'not at all' instead of just 'not'

Added preposition ب with kasra (بِ) at the beginning to show how or by what means something happens

Plural form of 'father' - adds extra alifs and changes the word pattern

This pattern shows someone who does the action. The word is in genitive case because it comes after the word القوم (the people) in a possession relationship.
Adding ين makes the word plural, showing multiple people who do wrong
Adding ٱلْ makes the word definite, like adding 'the' in English
This is a basic word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'definitely' in English
Added ا at the end to show this is about 'we/us'

steps.m: and indeed we (are able to show you what We promise them)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This word connects to لَقٰدِرُونَ (capable/able) later in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing something is on top of, over, or upon something else
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound
Added an elongation mark (ٓ) to show this word connects to the next word نُّرِيَكَ (show you)

steps.m: that (introduces what Allah is capable of doing)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects two parts of a sentence, specifically here introducing what Allah is capable of showing/demonstrating. It works with نُّرِيَكَ (we show you) to explain what Allah has power over.
Added نُ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, and ِ in the middle to show it's happening now
Added shadda on ن and كَ at the end to show who receives the action (you)
In this ayah, مَا is being used as a relative pronoun meaning 'that which'. It refers to what Allah is showing/will show to the Prophet. It's the object of the verb نُّرِيَكَ (We show you).

steps.m: to make a promise or commitment

steps.n: The و from the root is dropped and نَ is added at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. The ِ (kasra) on ع and ُ (damma) at the end show this is happening in the present.
Added هُمْ at the end to show who receives the promise. This word connects to لَقٰدِرُونَ (surely capable) in the ayah to mean 'we are surely capable of what we promise them.'
This root carries the basic meaning of having ability or power over something
Changes the verb into a word describing someone who has this ability
Makes the word plural to show multiple people who are capable
Adds emphasis to show this is definitely true
Adds fatha at end to show this is in the nominative case
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to a command form by adding اِ at start and putting sukun marks on middle and end
Removed the hamza carrier from the alif since it's not needed
This is a special word that connects parts of a sentence. It's feminine, so it's used when referring to feminine things or ideas.

steps.m: with/by that which (connected to أَحْسَنُ - better)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show how something should be done. In this ayah, it's connected to the word أَحْسَنُ (better), showing how to respond in a better way.
This is a basic pronoun that means 'she' or 'it' for feminine things. In this ayah, it's the subject of أَحْسَنُ (better/best), showing what is better.

steps.m: to be good, beautiful, or excellent

steps.n: Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the start to make it mean 'more/better', like turning 'good' into 'better'. It's comparing good deeds with bad deeds (السَّيِّئَةَ) in this ayah.

steps.m: to harm or cause bad effects

steps.n: The doubled letter makes it stronger - changes from 'harm' to 'very bad/evil'
Added ة to change from a general description of something bad to mean one specific bad deed
Added ال to specify this particular bad deed
Added fatha at end to show this is what's being repelled in the phrase 'repel the bad deed'
This is the basic form of the pronoun meaning 'we'
Added vowel marks to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb أَعْلَمُ (we know). The ُ (damma) at the end shows it's doing the action.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to elative form (comparative/superlative) to show 'most'. This word describes 'we' (نَحْنُ) in the ayah, showing that Allah is declaring His supreme knowledge.
This is a special word that either asks questions (like 'what?') or connects ideas (like 'that which'). In this ayah, it connects to يَصِفُونَ (they describe) to mean 'what they describe'
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is done 'with' or 'by' something. This connects to the verb يَصِفُونَ (they describe) to show how something is done.
Added ي at beginning to show it's happening now, with َ (fatha) and ِ (kasra) to connect the letters

steps.m: they describe (referring to those who attribute false qualities to Allah)
steps.n: Added ون at the end to show many people are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to those who incorrectly describe or attribute qualities to Allah.
Changed to a command form by adding ُ on first letter and dropping the middle weak letter و. This tells one person to do something.

steps.m: and speak! (command to Prophet ﷺ to say: رَّبِّ أَعُوذُ بِكَ)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this command with what came before it. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to say the words that follow.
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'one who does this action intensively'
Adding kasra at the end makes this a call/address to one's Lord
Changed root letters into a verb by adding the 'oo' sound (damma) in the middle
Added أَ at beginning to show first person present tense (I)
Added damma at end to show this is the main verb after قُل (say)
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'by' or 'with'

steps.m: by/with you (referring to Allah in أَعُوذُ بِكَ - I seek refuge in You)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this refers to 'you'. In this ayah, it's specifically referring to Allah since the person is seeking refuge in Him.
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'I seek refuge' (أَعُوذُ) with what we seek refuge from - 'the evil suggestions' (هَمَزٰتِ)

steps.m: to poke, prod, or whisper evil thoughts

steps.n: Added vowels (َ) to make it 'he poked' in past tense
Added ة to make it a noun for one instance of the action
Changed the ة to an alif maqsurah (ٰ) as part of forming the plural
Added the feminine plural ending ات (with kasra because it's possessed by 'the devils' that follows it)

steps.m: one who is distant and rebellious (satan/devil)
steps.n: Root letters got extra letters to make it mean 'one who is rebellious' instead of just 'being rebellious'
Makes the word refer to multiple devils/satans instead of just one
Adds ال to point to specific devils/satans. The ل merges into the ش making it doubled (شّ)
Adds a kasra (ِ) at the end to show these devils own or possess something (in this case, the whispers)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ (a-) at the start changes 'he sought protection' to 'I seek protection' in the present tense

steps.m: and I seek protection (from يَحْضُرُونِ - their presence)
steps.n: The و (and) was added at the start to connect this word to what came before it
This is a helper word (preposition) that shows seeking protection or help in this context
Added 'ka' to show who is being addressed (you). This word completes the meaning of أَعُوذُ (I seek refuge) by showing with whom refuge is sought.
Doubling the middle letter and adding fatha creates a noun for someone who does this action intensively

steps.m: my Lord (being called upon in أَعُوذُ بِكَ)
steps.n: Adding kasra at the end shows possession (my) and also shows this is being used as a form of address since it comes after أَعُوذُ بِكَ (I seek refuge in You)
This is a connecting word that joins two ideas together. Here it connects 'I seek refuge with You' to 'they come to me', showing what the person is seeking refuge from.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: they come to me (object of أَعُوذُ)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end which is a shortened form of ني meaning 'me/to me'. This word is the object of أَعُوذُ (I seek refuge) in this ayah.
This is the basic form of the particle that shows a limit or endpoint
Adding the shadda (doubled letter) makes the meaning stronger
Adding ى at the end shows this word relates to reaching a limit or endpoint
The small alif (ٰ) and the small vertical line (ٓ) show this connects to the word إِذَا (when) that comes after it, making the meaning 'until when'
This is the basic form of the word that refers to a specific point in time
Adding ا at the end makes it refer to future conditions or hypothetical situations. In this ayah, it's used to talk about the moment death comes to someone.
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he came'
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after ج
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make it readable
Added fatha at the end to prepare for connection with next part

steps.m: one of them (when death comes to)
steps.n: Added هُمُ at the end to show this refers to 'one of them'. In this ayah, it's talking about when death comes to one of them (the disbelievers).
Added vowels to turn the action of dying into the concept of death itself. This word is doing the action of 'coming' in the phrase 'death came to him'.
Added ال (the) at start and ُ at end to show 'the death' is doing the action
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form before the weak letter change

steps.m: he said (before رَبِّ ٱرْجِعُونِ - 'My Lord, return me')
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it has a fatha and comes between two fathas. This is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و in the middle).
The doubled middle letter shows this describes someone who does this action perfectly
Adding kasra at the end makes this a call/plea to one's Lord
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he returned' to 'return!' by adding alif at start and changing vowels - it's now a command to one person
Changed from singular command to plural by adding ون, which makes it a request from multiple people
Added ِ at the end to make it a stronger plea or request, like saying 'please return me!' instead of just 'return me'

The kasra (ِ) is added to prepare for attaching the pronoun
Added the first person pronoun ي (I) at the end. This word connects to أَعْمَلُ (I do/work) in the ayah, showing who might do the action.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added أ prefix to make it 'I' form and ُ at end to show present tense
Changed alif to alif with line above (same pronunciation)

steps.m: a good deed (object of أَعْمَلُ - I do/perform)

steps.m: in what (referring to what was left behind)
steps.n: Added ما which means 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it refers to the good deeds the person wishes they had done in their previous life.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added sukun to prepare for the suffix
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action. In this ayah, this refers to what the speaker left behind in their previous life.
Connected the root letters and added shadda to make the meaning stronger
Added long alif at the end to add even more emphasis to the denial

Added kasra at the end to show it's being used after a preposition or as a possessed noun
Changed the ending to show something belongs to/comes after something else
Added هِم to show it means 'them' (masculine plural)

steps.m: to blow, to sound (specifically related to horn/trumpet)
to be blown (passive voice of horn/trumpet)

Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and make a past-tense verb

Added vowel marks to make the basic word for 'day'
Added fatha at the end to connect it with the next part
Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that' day (referring to the Day of Judgment mentioned in the previous ayah)
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يَتَسَآءَلُونَ (they ask each other) that comes after it

Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ prefix and ا after first root letter to show mutual action between people
Changed from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and ُ at end - shows asking each other back and forth

steps.m: those (far) (referring to the people whose scales are heavy)

steps.m: so those (far) (referring to the people whose scales are heavy)

Added مُ at the start to show who does the action, and ِ under ل to complete the pattern
Added ون at the end to show we're talking about multiple people, and ُ before it to connect the sounds

steps.m: the ones who achieve success (they are هُمُ/those)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he became very light (subject of مَوٰزِينُهُۥ)

steps.m: it became very light (subject: مَوٰزِينُهُۥ - his scales)

Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

Changed the pattern to show many scales instead of just one

steps.m: so those (referring to those whose scales are light)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what was mentioned before (their scales being light)
Starting with the basic demonstrative word and changing it to make it relative
Adding ال makes it specifically refer to someone or something
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people
Adding the proper Arabic vowel marks and changing ال to ٱ. This word is the subject (doer) of خَسِرُوٓا۟ (they lost) in this ayah.

steps.m: to lose something valuable/to suffer a loss

steps.n: Adding vowels to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'to lose'
Added وا at the end to show a group of people (they) did this action. This word connects to أَنفُسَهُمْ (their souls) which shows what they lost.
The pattern creates a noun meaning 'self' or 'soul' - something precious that breathes. This word is what is being lost in the phrase 'they lost their souls'.
Added أ at the start and changed the middle vowel to ُ to make it plural, like changing 'foot' to 'feet' in English
Added َهُمْ at the end to show these souls belong to them, like adding 'their' in English
This is a basic preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it shows they are in Hell (جَهَنَّمَ).
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the proper noun for Hell in Arabic

steps.m: Hell (as the object being referred to)

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding alif after first root letter and kasra after second root letter creates a word meaning 'someone who stays forever'. This describes the people mentioned earlier (أولئك) in the verse.

steps.m: ones who will stay forever (describes أولئك)
steps.n: Adding ُونَ shows we're talking about many people. The small alif (ٰ) is just a writing style that means the same as a regular alif.

steps.m: to burn or scorch with intense heat

Changed from past to present tense by adding تَ prefix and ُ ending
Connected the root letters together to form the basic noun for a single face
Changes the vowel pattern to make it plural, showing many faces
Adds fatha to show this word is receiving the action of burning/scorching from تَلْفَحُ (scorches)
Adds pronoun suffix to show these faces belong to them

steps.m: to shine, give off light or heat
fire (that burns and gives off light)
steps.n: Changes the concept of giving light/heat into the thing that produces it (fire)

steps.m: the intense fire (that will burn their faces)

Added ها at the end with fatha (َ) to show it refers back to the feminine word النَّارُ (the Fire) mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: to show teeth in a frown/grimace

Changed the word to show who is doing the action

steps.m: those who are frowning/grimacing (subjects of تَلْفَحُ)

This turns a statement into a question that expects a 'yes' answer
Adding لَ makes the question more emphatic
Adding مْ makes the question negative. In this ayah, it's asking 'Were not My verses recited to you?' expecting the answer 'Yes, they were.'
Added damma to show basic command form
Added ت to make it 'you' form in present tense

steps.m: to show, make clear, or demonstrate

steps.n: Changed from a verb root meaning 'show' into a noun meaning 'sign' by adding an alif after hamza and taa marboota at the end
Changed singular to plural by removing ة and adding ات
Added kasra to show it's being possessed by something
Added ي to show these are 'my' signs
Changed ي to ى which is just a spelling variation for 'my'

steps.m: to recite or read out loud

steps.m: is being recited (referring to ءَايٰتِى/My verses)

steps.m: to deny something known to be true

steps.m: Our Lord! (being called upon in قَالُوا)

steps.m: to overcome or defeat something by being stronger

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added ت with sukun at the end to show that شِقْوَتُنَا (our wretchedness) is what did the overcoming. Since شِقْوَتُنَا is feminine, we use the feminine form of the verb.
This is a basic preposition that shows something is physically or metaphorically on top of, over, or affecting something else - like saying 'on' in English
Added ي and sukun (ْ) to prepare for attaching the pronoun. This is just a spelling change that happens before adding نا.
Added نَا which means 'us'. In this ayah, عَلَيْنَا shows who was overcome - our misfortune overcame us.
Connected the root letters with vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun that means 'one occurrence of being wretched'
نَا shows the wretchedness belongs to us. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb غَلَبَتْ (overcame).

steps.m: to be in a state or condition

steps.n: Connected the letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed the vowel to ُ and added نَّا to show 'we' did the action in the past
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it in the ayah

steps.m: to stand upright or rise up

steps.n: Changes verb root into a noun meaning a group of people
Adds ًا to show this noun is being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: to wander off the right path

steps.n: Basic past tense form with vowels added to show 'he' did the action

steps.m: one who wanders off the path
steps.n: Changed from a past-tense verb (he went astray) to a noun describing someone who goes astray

steps.m: one who is completely lost from the path
steps.n: The longer آ sound shows the straying is more complete or intense

steps.m: those who are completely lost (describes قَوْمًا/people)
steps.n: Changed to plural form to match the word قَوْمًا (people) it describes
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures in a complete and perfect way
Added نَا to show this is our Lord who nurtures us
The extra alif shows we are calling out to our Lord
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the beginning makes the verb causative - making someone do the action instead of doing it yourself
Changed from 'he made go out' to a command telling someone to 'make go out!'

steps.m: make us go out! (a plea to رَبَّنَآ - our Lord)
steps.n: Added نا to show who is being made to exit - in this case 'us'. This is part of a plea to Allah (رَبَّنَآ - our Lord)
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'. In this ayah, it's used by people in hellfire asking to be taken 'from' there.
Added هَا which refers back to hellfire (feminine in Arabic). The people are asking to be taken out from it.
This is a basic conditional particle that introduces a possible situation. In this ayah, it introduces the condition 'if we return (to evil)'.

steps.m: and if (connecting to عُدْنَا - we return)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to connect this condition with what came before. It shows this is a response or result of the previous statement.

steps.m: to go back or return repeatedly

steps.n: The root letters got vowels to make past tense 'he returned'. The middle letter و turned into ا, which often happens with these types of words.
Changed from past tense 'he returned' to a command 'return!' by dropping the middle ا and changing vowels. Like saying 'go!' instead of 'he went'
Adding نَا shows that 'we' did the action. This word appears in a conditional sentence after إِنْ (if), making it mean 'if we return'.
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before, connecting it to the previous statement رَبَّنَآ أَخْرِجْنَا مِنْهَا (Our Lord, take us out from it)
Added نا at the end to show 'we' are the ones being talked about. This connects to the next word ظٰلِمُونَ (wrongdoers) to complete the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who acts unjustly (doer of wrong)
steps.n: This pattern shows someone who does the action regularly or as their characteristic. The word is part of إِنَّا ظٰلِمُونَ (indeed we are wrongdoers) in this ayah.
Adding ون makes it plural for multiple wrongdoers. The ُ (damma) comes before ون because this word is the predicate of إِنَّ.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense. This word is connected to what follows - he is the one giving the command ٱخْسَـُٔوا۟ (be driven away in disgrace).
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي as middle root letter)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Changed from past tense to a command by adding alif and making first letter silent

steps.m: be driven away! (command to group) (Allah's response to كَافِرُونَ)
steps.n: Changed from telling one person to go away to telling a group to go away by adding وا at the end. This is Allah's harsh command to the disbelievers.
Basic preposition meaning 'in'
Added ها which refers back to Hell (جهنم), which is feminine in Arabic. This word appears in a conversation about the people of Hell being told to stay in it and not speak.
Added fatha to show proper pronunciation
Added لا which means 'not'. This negates the verb تُكَلِّمُونِ (you speak) that comes after it in the ayah, making it 'you do not speak'
Doubling the middle letter shows speaking is done with intensity or repeatedly to someone
Adding تُ at start changes it from 'speaking to someone' to 'being spoken to'
Changed from passive (being spoken to) to active (you all speaking) and added ون to show multiple people doing it

steps.m: you (all) speak to me (don't speak to me!)
steps.n: Added ِ (kasra) at the end to mean 'to me', changing it from general 'you all speak' to specifically 'you all speak to me'. Used here as 'don't speak to me!'
This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more emphasized, like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' in English
Added هُۥ at the end to show who is being talked about. This connects back to the group (فَرِيقٌ) mentioned in the next part of the verse.
Added vowel marks (fathas) to change from the root meaning 'to be' into 'he was/existed' in the past tense
Changed from basic past tense to common past tense by adding alif - this is a special change that happens with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle)
Connected the root letters and added ي to show this is a distinct group that has been separated from others
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject doing the action in يَقُولُونَ (they say)
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or what group something belongs to. Here it connects to عِبَادِى (My servants) to show which group is being talked about.
Added a shadda (double mark) on the م to show it connects strongly to the word before it. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'to worship' into plural noun meaning 'worshippers'
Adds ى to show possession (my) and ِ because it follows the word مِنْ (from)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and changed vowels due to the weak middle letter و

steps.m: they speak/say (subject of a group speaking to their Lord)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people are speaking. In this ayah, this refers to a group of people addressing their Lord (رَبَّنَآ)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense form
The doubled letter shows intensity and completeness of the nurturing quality
Added نَا to show it belongs to "us" (the speakers). This word is what is being said by the group mentioned in يَقُولُونَ (they say).
The extra alif (آ) shows this is being used to call out to Allah directly
Added hamza with fatha and alif to make past tense verb. This word is part of what the servants say (يَقُولُونَ) in this ayah.
Added 'naa' (ا with shadda on ن) to show multiple people believing
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he forgave' to 'forgive!' by adding 'i' at start and changing middle vowel - makes it a command

steps.m: so forgive! (in response to what was said before)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a response to what was mentioned in the previous part of the verse
Added fatha to show how to pronounce the letter ل

steps.m: for us/to us (speaking to رَبّ, our Lord)
steps.n: Added نَا which means 'us' to show who the action is for. In this ayah, a group of believers are speaking to their Lord asking for forgiveness.
Changed to a command by adding alif at the start - telling someone to 'show mercy!'
Added نَا meaning 'us'. This is a command/imperative verb, not a word that takes case endings.
Added conjunction و meaning 'and'
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'you' - in this context it is addressing Allah directly as the believers make dua (supplication)
Added وَ meaning 'and' to connect this with the previous phrase where the believers are asking Allah for mercy (وَٱرْحَمْنَا - and have mercy on us)
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make a noun meaning good/better/best
Added damma at the end to show this word describes الرَّاحِمِينَ (the Most Merciful) in the phrase 'best of those who show mercy'
This pattern shows who does the action regularly or as a characteristic
Changes the word to refer to multiple people who show mercy
Adding shadda intensifies the meaning, emphasizing the quality of mercy
Adding ال makes it refer to specific ones who show mercy, not just any ones
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding ت with shadda and changing initial vowel shows doing something for oneself

steps.m: and so he took for himself
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this follows from previous events
Adding تُم changes it from 'he took' to 'you all took' in the past

steps.m: and so you all took them (as سِخْرِيًّا mockery)
steps.n: Adding وهُمْ shows who was taken/adopted. The word سِخْرِيًّا explains they were taken as objects of mockery.

steps.m: to mock or make fun of something

steps.n: Changed from a root meaning 'to mock' into a noun meaning 'mockery' by adding vowel marks (i) and (sukoon)
Adds يّ to make it an adjective meaning 'related to mockery'
Adds tanween (double fatHa) to make it an adverb meaning 'in a mocking way'

steps.m: as an object of mockery (acted on by اتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ)
steps.n: Added ًا to show this word describes how the believers were treated. This connects to the verb اتَّخَذْتُمُوهُمْ (you took/treated them).
This is a particle (like 'to' or 'until' in English) that shows something continues up to a certain point
Added fatha on first letter and shadda with fatha on second letter to show proper pronunciation
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) with a small alif above it (ٓ) at the end. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Transforms from basic verb 'to forget' to causative 'to make forget'
Adds plural masculine suffix to indicate 'they' as the subject
Adding كم as object pronoun (you plural)
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (he remembered) to a noun (remembrance) by changing the vowels
Adding an 'i' sound at the end to connect it to the next word (like saying 'of')

steps.m: my remembrance (object of أَنسَوْكُمْ/made you forget)
steps.n: Adds first person singular possessive pronoun suffix. This word is what was made forgotten in the phrase 'made you forget my remembrance'
The weak letter و is dropped, and damma is added on ك to show its original presence
Adding تُ shows this happened in the past and was done by 'you'
Adding م changes from single 'you' to multiple people
Adding وَ connects this to the previous statement about how they took others in mockery
Added shadda for emphasis since this word follows a vowel sound in the previous word
هم shows who is being referred to - them (a group)
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding تَ at start and changing vowels
Added ون to show multiple people are laughing
ون suffix makes this plural - 'you all' instead of just 'you'
Adding a shadda (doubling mark) on the ن changes this from 'if' to a word that adds emphasis to what comes after it

steps.m: indeed I (speaking before جَزَيْتُهُمُ - I rewarded them)
steps.n: Added ي at the end to show 'I' am speaking. The ي combines with the نّ making it نِّ. When ي comes at the end of a word, it's written as ى
Connected the root letters and added fatha marks. The final ي changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.
The ى changes back to يْ before adding endings
Added تُ to show that 'I' did the action in the past

steps.m: I rewarded them (subject of هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُمُ to show who received the reward. This word connects to هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (they are the successful ones) later in the ayah.
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to make it a noun meaning 'a day'

steps.m: the day (object of جَزَيْتُ - I rewarded)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the day') and َ at the end shows it's receiving the action of the verb جَزَيْتُ (I rewarded)
This is a preposition that connects actions to their causes or means. Here it connects to the word صَبَرُوٓا۟ (they were patient)

steps.m: because of what/with what (they were patient)
steps.n: مَا means 'what' and connects this to صَبَرُوٓا۟ (they were patient) in the next part of the sentence. Together it means 'because of their patience'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added damma at the end to show this is in the nominative case
Added وا to show that a group of people did the action. This word connects to أَنَّهُمْ (that they) in the ayah.

steps.m: that/because (connects to صَبَرُوا - they were patient)
steps.n: This particle introduces a new clause explaining the result of their patience mentioned in the previous ayah
هُمْ is the 3rd person masculine plural pronoun suffix
This is the basic form of the third person masculine plural pronoun meaning 'they'
Added damma marks to show this is a separate pronoun used for emphasis. In this ayah, it emphasizes that these people are indeed 'the successful ones' (الْفَآئِزُونَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes verb 'achieved success' into 'person who achieves success' by adding alif after first letter
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple successful people
The word gained 'the' (ال) at the start to show these are THE successful ones being talked about
Added short vowels 'a' to make it mean 'he said' in the past
Changed middle weak letter و to ا, which is common for hollow verbs

steps.m: he said (asking about كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ - how long you remained)
steps.n: The alif is written with an overbar (ٰ) as a shorter form. This is just a writing variation and doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the question word used to ask about quantities

steps.m: how long (asking about لَبِثْتُمْ - you remained)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show exact pronunciation. In this ayah, it's asking about how long they remained on earth.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to stay'
Added sukun to prepare for adding endings

steps.m: you all stayed/remained (Allah is asking how long)
steps.n: Added تُمْ to show many people are being addressed. This is part of Allah asking how long people stayed.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it shows location within 'the earth'.
Added the kasra vowel mark (ِ) to show how to pronounce the word correctly
Basic root letters got vowel marks (fatha and sukun) to make the common noun meaning 'earth'
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ِ at the end because it follows the preposition فِى (in)
Connected the root letters that represent the basic concept of counting or numbering
Adding the vowel marks makes it a complete past tense verb meaning 'he counted'
The root meaning 'to pass' becomes a feminine noun 'year' by changing the last letter to ة. This ة makes it feminine like many other nouns.
Changes to plural form by replacing ة with ينَ and changing vowels to kasra (ِ). This word is connected to لَبِثْتُمْ (you stayed) in the ayah, asking about how many years they remained.
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed from قَوَلَ by replacing weak letter و with ا
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This word is connected to لَبِثْنَا (we remained) as part of what they are saying.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to stay'

steps.m: we stayed/remained (subject of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Added نَا to show 'we' did the action. The sukun (ْ) is added before نَا according to Arabic grammar rules. This word tells what 'we' did after 'they said' (قَالُوا).
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun meaning 'day'

steps.m: a day (object of لَبِثْنَا - we remained)
steps.n: The ًا at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb لَبِثْنَا (we remained) and answers 'how long?' they remained
This is a basic connecting particle that shows options or alternatives. In this ayah, it connects 'a day' with 'part of a day', showing uncertainty about the exact time.
Added the vowel mark fatha (َ) on أ and sukun (ْ) on و to show correct pronunciation
Connected the root letters together with basic vowel marks to form a noun meaning a single part or portion

steps.m: a part of (possessed by يَوْمٍ - day)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is being possessed by يَوْمٍ (day), forming the phrase 'part of a day'
Basic noun pattern with fatha-sukoon creates the noun 'day' from the concept of measuring time

steps.m: a day (acted on by لَبِثْنَا - we remained)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite ('a day' not 'the day') and is being acted upon by the verb لَبِثْنَا (we remained).
Changed root letters into command form by adding marks (sukun-fatha-sukun) to tell one person to ask
Added فَ at beginning to show this command follows from what came before

steps.m: so ask! (command to one person, connected to ٱلْعَآدِّينَ)
steps.n: Changed final vowel to kasra to connect with the next word (ٱلْعَآدِّينَ - the counters/reckoners)
Changed from basic root letters to a past tense verb by adding vowels and doubling the middle letter (shown by shadda)
Added alif after first letter to show who does the action
Added ين to make it plural (more than one counter)
Added ال to make it definite (specific counters) and changed the alif to show length. This word is being acted upon by the verb فَسْـَٔلِ (ask)
Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it mean 'he said' in the past
Changed from قَوَلَ to قَالَ by replacing the weak letter و with ا
The alif is written as a small alif above the ق for brevity in writing, but pronounced the same way
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it's used with لَّبِثْتُمْ (you remained) to express 'if you remained'
Added vowels to make a past tense verb
Added تُم at the end to show that many people are being spoken to
Added emphasis to the first letter with shadda
This is a basic particle used for making exceptions. It has a fixed form and does not come from a root.
Added shadda and alif to complete the exception particle. In this ayah, it introduces the exception 'only a little while' (قَلِيلًا) to contrast with what was previously mentioned.

steps.m: to be small in amount or number

steps.n: The ي makes it into an adjective describing something that has the quality of being small in amount
Added ًا at the end to show this word describes how long they remained. In this ayah, it's saying they only stayed 'a little while'
Basic conditional word that introduces an imaginary situation that didn't happen, like saying 'if only' or 'what if'
Added emphasis by doubling the L sound. This happens when لو comes right after certain words like إِن, making the 'if' stronger
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more certain
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to
Added vowels (a) to make it past tense 'he was'
The و drops out and the first vowel changes to ُ because this is a hollow verb (has و in middle)
Added تُمْ to show this refers to a group being spoken to in the past
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adds تَ at beginning to show present tense 'you' and changes vowels to show ongoing action
Adds ون at the end to show that multiple people (all male or mixed group) are doing the action
Adding vowels to make a basic past tense verb meaning 'he thought'
Adding تُ changes it from 'he thought' to 'I thought'
Changed from 'I' to 'you all' by adding تُمْ at the end
Added أَ to make it a question

steps.m: So did you all think/suppose? (connecting to أَنَّمَا خَلَقْنٰكُمْ عَبَثًا)
steps.n: Added فَ (meaning 'so/then') to connect this question with what follows about creation
This is an emphatic particle that introduces certainty to what follows

steps.m: that indeed (connects to خَلَقْنٰكُمْ - we created you)
steps.n: Adding ما makes the meaning more emphatic and connects it to what follows. Here it introduces the idea that follows - that Allah created us with purpose.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows someone did the action in the past.
Added sukun to prepare for attachments
Added نٰ to show 'we' did the creating. The alif after ن is written long to show it's stretched in pronunciation.
Added كُمْ to show who was created - it means 'you all' and shows the creation was done to many people

steps.m: to act without purpose or uselessly

steps.n: Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb form
Added ًا at the end to show this describes how something was done. In this ayah, it describes how Allah created us (خَلَقْنٰكُمْ) - suggesting it wasn't without purpose.
This is the base word that shows something is certain or emphasized
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added كُمْ at the end to show this refers to multiple people being spoken to
This is a preposition that shows direction or destination
Changed the ending to prepare for attachment to 'نا'. The ى changes to ي with sukun
Added نا which means 'us'. This word connects to تُرْجَعُونَ (you will be returned) to show who you will be returned to.
Basic negative particle that negates the verb تُرْجَعُونَ (you will be returned) that comes after it
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Adding basic past tense vowel pattern
Adding ت at the beginning and changing the vowels makes this passive - 'you are returned' instead of 'he returned'
Adds plural ending to show multiple people are being addressed (in this case, all humanity being returned to Allah)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb
Adding تَ at beginning shows doing action to oneself. The ى at end is because it's a weak verb ending in و
Added فَ (so/then) to connect with previous statement. Capital 'He' used because this refers to Allah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns
This is the unique name for The One True God in Islam. The ال makes it definite, the shadda emphasizes the meaning, and the ُ shows this is the subject of the sentence.
Changes from verb 'to rule' into noun meaning 'one who rules'
Added ٱلْ to show this refers to Allah, the one true king (as shown by the context لَآ إِلٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ - there is no god but Him)

steps.m: the King (as a title of Allah)
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject/doer in the sentence

steps.m: to be true, real, or right

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Added shadda (doubling mark) on ق to make it stronger
Adding ٱلْ makes it specific - THE truth, not just any truth
The ُ shows this word is the subject in the sentence
Basic negative particle that negates what comes after it. Here it negates إِلٰهَ (deity) to say 'there is no deity'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation
Added alif with madda to show elongated pronunciation

steps.m: to worship or take as a deity

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to worship' into noun meaning 'a god'
Adds fatha at end to show this is the object of a verb

steps.m: except (connects to لَا إِلٰهَ - 'there is no god')
steps.n: This is a basic particle that creates exceptions. Here it connects to the phrase 'there is no god' to mean 'there is no god except...'
This is the basic form of the third person masculine singular pronoun

steps.m: He (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) on ه and fatha (َ) on و to show correct pronunciation. In this ayah, this pronoun refers back to Allah mentioned earlier, emphasizing His unique divinity.

steps.m: to nurture, raise, develop, care for

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter shows this is someone who does this action perfectly and completely

steps.m: The Lord (subject of فَتَعٰلَى - so exalted is)
steps.n: The ُ ending shows this word is the subject being described as exalted in the ayah
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it a noun referring to the actual object
Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to make it specific - THE throne of Allah, not just any throne
The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this is possessed by رَبُّ (Lord) in the phrase 'Lord of the throne'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show this is a strong, permanent quality
Added ال to make it specific. This word describes العرش (the throne)

steps.m: of the noble/generous one (describing العرش/the throne)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end because this word follows and describes العرش (the throne)
This is a combination of two particles: و (and) + من (who/whoever)

steps.m: to call out to, invite, pray to

steps.n: The root letters were connected with fathas to make a past tense verb. The و at the end turned into ا because it's at the end of a word.
Added ي at start to show present tense. The middle letter gets sukun and the last vowel becomes damma with و to show it's ongoing action.
The sukun on middle letter shows this is a command-like form
This is a basic preposition that shows accompaniment. In this ayah, it connects to the word اللَّهِ (Allah) to mean 'with Allah'
The ل gets emphasized with a shadda to show the special importance of this name
The kasra at the end shows this connects to the word مَعَ (with) in the phrase 'with Allah'

steps.m: to be worshipped, to be taken as divine

steps.n: Root letters أ-ل-ه became the noun إِلَه meaning 'god' by adding special vowels
The small mark above shows the 'aa' sound is stretched out when saying the word

steps.m: a god (object of يَدْعُ - calls upon)
steps.n: Added tanween fatha to show this is the object being called upon in the phrase 'whoever calls upon a god'

steps.m: to come after, to be different from what's first

steps.n: This word is describing إِلٰهًا (god) in the phrase 'another god'. The special pattern with ءَا at the start and َ after each root letter makes it mean 'another' or 'different'.
Added a fatha mark at the end to match the word it describes (إِلٰهًا), showing they are connected in meaning
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed/prosper) that comes after it in this ayah, making it mean 'does not succeed'
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Changed the root letters into a noun by adding special marks (damma ُ and sukun ْ) to make it mean 'proof' instead of 'to prove'
Added alif (ا) to strengthen the meaning. This word is being negated by لا in the ayah, showing 'no proof'.
The alif was written as alif maksura (ٰ) and got a fatha mark (َ) since it's receiving the action
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam
Added the pronoun ه with damma (ُ) to show it means 'him/it'

Added the small و (ۥ) which is a pronunciation helper showing that the هُ is pronounced with a long 'u' sound. This word connects to بُرْهٰنَ (proof) in the ayah, showing there is no proof for him/it.
Added kasra (ِ) to help pronunciation
Added pronoun ه (him/it) to show what/who the preposition connects to. Here it refers back to برْهٰن (proof) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Added small و (ۦ) as a pronunciation helper for the هِ
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement
Added ما at the end to restrict or limit what comes after (meaning 'only')
This is the basic root meaning
Changes from a verb (to calculate) into a noun (calculation)
Adds هُۥ to show it belongs to 'him' and ُ to show this word is doing the action in the sentence

steps.m: to be present, to be near
steps.n: This word is used to show location or possession, like saying something is 'at' or 'with' someone
Added vowel marks to create a noun meaning 'presence' or 'nearness'

steps.m: at, with, near (of رَبِّهِۦٓ - his Lord)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to make it work as a preposition. In this ayah, it shows the location 'with/near his Lord' where the reckoning will be.
This is the basic root meaning
Connected the letters and added shadda to show intensity, making it mean 'the one who does this action perfectly'
Added kasra to show this word is owned by or connected to something else
Added هِۦٓ to show it means 'his Lord'. This whole phrase is possessed by the word عِندَ (with/at) in the ayah.
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger

steps.m: indeed he/it (connected to لَا يُفْلِحُ - will not succeed)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هُ (he/it) to show who the emphasis is about. The ۥ mark shows this is pronounced with a long vowel. This word connects to the verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed) in the next part of the ayah.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُفْلِحُ (succeed/prosper) that comes after it in the ayah, making it mean 'will not succeed'
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changes to present tense by adding يُ at start, changing vowels, and adding ُ at end. This word is negated by لا in the ayah, so it means 'he does not succeed'

steps.m: to reject or cover up truth
steps.n: This is the basic root meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed to show who does the action
Made it plural to show many people

steps.m: the disbelievers (subject of لَا يُفْلِحُ - will not succeed)
steps.n: Added 'the' at the beginning and lengthened the 'a' sound. This word is the subject of لَا يُفْلِحُ (will not succeed) in this ayah.
The weak letter و is dropped in the command form. Added sukun at the end to show it's a complete command.
The و connects this command to the previous phrase 'My Lord, forgive', making it part of a sequence of requests
Doubling the ب adds emphasis to show complete mastery/lordship
When calling out to Allah with وَقُل (and say), we add a shadda at the start to show we are directly speaking to Him, like saying 'O Lord!'

steps.m: O my Lord! (calling upon for ٱغْفِرْ وَٱرْحَمْ - forgive and have mercy)
steps.n: Adding kasra to show possession (my Lord). This is part of the prayer phrase 'O my Lord, forgive and have mercy'

steps.m: to forgive, pardon, cover up faults

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fathas to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it a command form - sukun on first letter, kasra on middle letter, and sukun at end

steps.m: forgive! (command to one person) (connected to رَّبِّ - O Lord)
steps.n: Added hamzat al-wasl at beginning to help pronunciation since Arabic words can't start with a sukun
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed from 'he showed kindness' to a command 'show kindness!' by adding ٱ at start and making it shorter
Added وَ (and) to connect this command with the previous phrase رَّبِّ ٱغْفِرْ (My Lord, forgive)
This is a pronoun with no root

steps.m: and you (addressing Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding و at the start connects this word to what came before. Here it's part of the phrase 'and You are the best of those who show mercy'
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the comparative/superlative form

steps.m: best (of الرَّاحِمِينَ - those who show mercy)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show this describes أَنتَ (You) in the phrase 'You are the best of those who show mercy'
Changed from basic root meaning 'mercy' into a word meaning 'someone who shows mercy' by adding alif after ra and kasra under ha
Adding ين makes it plural (more than one). This word is in the genitive case because it comes after خَيْرُ (best) in a possession relationship, meaning 'best of those who have mercy'.
Adding ال makes it definite. The ر gets a shadda because it comes after ال.

steps.m: the most merciful ones (after خَيْرُ/best of)
steps.n: This word comes after خَيْرُ (best) in the phrase خَيْرُ ٱلرَّاحِمِينَ meaning 'best of those who show mercy'
